coredata.dc:title: Ultra-stable tellurium-doped carbon quantum dots for cell protection and near-infrared photodynamic application

coredata.dc:description: It is important to regulate the concentration of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells since they play important roles in metabolism. Thus, developing nanoreagents to control the ROS is critical. Herein, tellurium-doped carbon quantum dots (Te-CDs) were developed by a simple and efficient hydrothermal method, which can scavenge H2O2 to protect cells under ambient condition, but generate ·OH under 808 nm irradiation as photodynamic application. This contribution presented a kind of novel CDs with dual-functions, which can potentially regulate ROS under different conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reactive oxygen species regulation
Tellurium
Carbon quantum dots
Scavenge free-radical
Photodynamic effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of Te-CDs
Cytotoxicity, ROS scavenging and photodynamic application in vitro

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthetic procedure of Te-CDs. (b) AFM topography image and height profile of Te-CDs. (c) HR-TEM image of Te-CDs. (d) XPS spectrum of Te-CDs. (e) High-resolution XPS spectra of Te 3d.

(a) Excitation-dependent photoluminescence spectra. (b) The fluorescence intensity of Te-CDs with different concentration of H2O2. (c) Fluorescence redox cycles of Te-CDs (250 μg mL−1, 3 mL) after H2O2 (0.5 mol L−1, 20 μL) and GSH (1 mol L−1, 10 μL) were alternatively added. (d) The decay curves of DPBF and Te-CDs (125 μg mL−1) solution under 808 nm irradiation at 1.40 W cm−2. (e) Normalized ROS generation of Te-CDs (125 μg mL−1) and ICG (125 μg mL−1) under 808 nm irradiation (1.40 W cm−2) using DPBF as a probe. (f) The absorption intensities of Te-CDs after irradiation with 808 nm at 0.72, 1.40, 2.00 W cm−2 for 30 min.

(a) ESR spectra of H2O2 with or without Te-CDs using TEMP as spin-trapping agent. (b) ESR spectra of Te-CDs in aqueous solution with or without 808 nm laser irradiation using DEMP as spin-trapping agent.

(a) Cell viability of L929 and HeLa cells incubated with Te-CDs at various concentrations for 24 h. (b) Cell viability of L929 cells incubated with various concentrations of Te-CDs in the presence 5 ppm H2O2. (c) Fluorescence intensity of L929 cells incubated with PBS, 5 ppm H2O2, Te-CDs (125 μg mL−1) and 5 ppm H2O2 + Te-CDs (125 μg mL−1) using DCFH-DA as a ROS probe. (d) Cell viability of HeLa cells incubated with Te-CDs at various concentrations under 808 nm laser irradiation (1.4 W cm−2). All the data are expressed as means ± s.d (n = 6 for (a, b, c), n = 3 for (d)). Statistical significance: ***P < 0.001.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of fluorescence field and merged field showing HeLa cells in PBS and incubated with 125 μg mL−1 Te-CDs with or without 808 nm laser irradiation. (a) DCFH-DA was used as the ROS probe. Scale bars = 40 µm. (b) calcein-AM (live cells, green fluorescence) and PI (dead cells, red fluorescence) were used as the probe. Scale bars = 200 µm.

head.ce:title: Ultra-stable tellurium-doped carbon quantum dots for cell protection and near-infrared photodynamic application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Materials Science and Opto-electronic Technology, College of Materials Science and Opto-electronic Technology & CAS Center for Excellence in Topological Quantum Computation & CAS Key Laboratory of Vacuum Physics, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Materials Science and Opto-electronic Technology
College of Materials Science and Opto-electronic Technology & CAS Center for Excellence in Topological Quantum Computation & CAS Key Laboratory of Vacuum Physics
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Materials Science and Opto-electronic Technology, College of Materials Science and Opto-electronic Technology & CAS Center for Excellence in Topological Quantum Computation & CAS Key Laboratory of Vacuum Physics, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE), Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials (SICAM), Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials (SICAM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE), Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials (SICAM), Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is important to regulate the concentration of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells since they play important roles in metabolism. Thus, developing nanoreagents to control the ROS is critical. Herein, tellurium-doped carbon quantum dots (Te-CDs) were developed by a simple and efficient hydrothermal method, which can scavenge H2O2 to protect cells under ambient condition, but generate ·OH under 808 nm irradiation as photodynamic application. This contribution presented a kind of novel CDs with dual-functions, which can potentially regulate ROS under different conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS), the intermediate of metabolic reactions, play a vital role in cells [1,2]. In general, low concentrations of ROS are critical for signal transduction and redox homeostasis in normal cells [3–5], since lipids, proteins and DNA can be damaged irretrievably when the concentration of ROS are too high to form oxidative stress [5–7]. Therefore, it is important to avoid ROS accumulation to protect cells and prolong lives. In the other side, ROS can be employed as reagents for cancer therapy, which is so-called photodynamic therapy (PDT). Not surprisingly, PDT is an attractive choice for cancer therapy due to the selectively treatment and minimally invasive modalities [8–13]. During PDT, the photosensitizers react with oxygen in tissues to produce ROS under light irradiation to kill cancer cells [14,15]. Thus, precisely tuning concentration of ROS in cells is highly important for lives.

Different materials, including inorganic nanomaterials [16,17], organic nanoagents [18–23], and metal organic framework (MOF) [24,25], have been developed to consume ROS to protect cells or generate ROS for cancer therapy. For example, Guo’s group prepared black phosphorus nanosheets loading doxorubicin to realize multimodal cancer therapy [26]. Xu’s group [27,28] developed several ROS-responsive polymers as intracellular ROS scavenger to protect cells from oxidative stress damage. Lin’s group [29,30] reported the chlorin-based MOFs for effective tumor therapy. Thus, it is important to develop novel materials that can regulate ROS concentration under different conditions.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), a novel class of quantum dots made from carbon materials, have been widely employed for biological applications due to their great biocompatibility, easily functionalization, highly tunable optical property and chemical inertness [31–35]. Recently, tuning physicochemical properties of CDs through heteroatom (e.g., N, P, S, Se) doping have gained much attention [36,37]. For example, Ge et al. [38,39] employed thiophene functional group as sulfur sources to dope CDs, resulting in multimodal diagnosis and phototherapy efficacy. Yu et al. [40,41] employed selenium (Se) to dope CDs to function as a redox fluorescent switch due to its excellent performance in antioxidant. Also, Zhang et al. [42] used the Se- and tellurium (Te)-containing CDs as superoxide anion radical fluorescence probe in mice exercise and emotional changes, which suggested that Te doped CDs can consume intracellular ROS as antioxidants.

Herein, novel Te-doped CDs (Te-CDs) were prepared via a simple hydrothermal method by using tellurocystine as carbon sources, supported by atomic force microscopy (AFM), the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) and fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Te-CDs are ultra-stable and biocompatible, which can scavenge H2O2 for protecting normal cells in ambient condition, while generate ·OH for killing cancer cells under 808 nm laser irradiation. This suggested the Te-CDs can realize dual functions in cells under different conditions.

The precursor, tellurocystine, was synthesized according to the literature [43]. Then tellurocystine (10 mg) was dissolved in deionized water (10 mL) at pH around 9 for good solubility. The solution was then heated to 60 °C and kept for 24 h. The resulting brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 r min−1 for 10 min, after which the supernatant was collected and dialyzed with the dialysis membrane. Finally, the yellow Te-CDs solution was obtained.

UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a Gary 60 UV–vis Spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured by a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. The size and morphology of Te-CDs were analyzed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) instruments (Malvern ZetaSizer Nano ZS90) and Tecnai G2F20S-TWIN TEM, NTEGRA Solaris AFM, Via-Reflex Raman spectroscopy, Nicolet Impact 410 FT-IR, Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi XPS and Agilent ICPOES730 ICP-OES. Quantum yield was measured with an Edinburgh FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon arc lamp.

Mouse fibroblast L929 cells were cultured in Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI-1640) media, and human HeLa cells were cultivated in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), which both contain 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin in the incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2). More experimental details can be found in the Supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of Te-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: AFM, HR-TEM and DLS were used to investigate the morphologies and size distributions of Te-CDs. Fig. 1 b showed that Te-CDs possess the consistent diameters around 2 nm. Also, a typical lattice spacing of (0 0 2) interlayers (0.29 nm) was observed in HR-TEM image (Fig. 1c), suggesting their crystalline properties. The DLS results showed that average hydrodynamic Te-CDs diameters followed Gaussian distribution ranging at (3.2 ± 0.3) nm (Fig. S1 online), which were consistent with AFM and HR-TEM measurements.

Raman spectroscopy, XPS, ICP-OES and FT-IR were performed to investigate the structure and chemical composition of Te-CDs. As shown in Fig. S2 (online), there are two broad peaks at 1380 and 1568 cm−1, which were reasonably assigned to the disordered D band and crystalline G band, respectively [44]. XPS images (Fig. 1d,e) showed the typical peaks of C 1s, N 1s, Te 3d and O 1s, suggesting the Te-CDs contain carbon, nitrogen, tellurium, and oxygen. The doping concentration of Te element in Te-CDs was determined to be 41.15 wt% using ICP-OES. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3 online) provided the surface chemical bonds features. The strong and broad peak ranging from 3000 to 3700 cm−1 can be ascribed to O–H or N–H stretching modes, while the peaks at 1590 and 1470 cm−1 were assigned to the C = C stretching mode of aromatic domain. Furthermore, the sharp peak at 1383 cm−1 and other peaks between 900 and 1200 cm−1 were assigned to the C–H bending vibration and the stretching vibrations of C-O, C-N, and C-Te, respectively. Zeta potential measurements showed that the particle surface charge of Te-CDs (Fig. S4 online) is −23 meV, suggesting their excellent colloidal stability in water [45].

Te-CDs demonstrated different luminescent peaks when they were excited at different wavelengths (Fig. 2 a). This excitation dependent emission property is ascribed to multiple luminescent centers of different functional groups on the surface of CDs [46]. Unlike the reported glowing carbon dots [34,47], the absolute photoluminescence quantum yield in water is only 3.96%, suggesting radiative decay is not the dominant route for the exciting state. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity increased when H2O2 or ·OH was added into solution (Fig. 2b and Fig. S8 online), which may be ascribed to the oxidization of Te atoms to Te = O [42]. This observation suggested that Te-CDs may be excellent ROS scavengers. Furthermore, the emission intensity decreased by adding the reductant glutathione (GSH), indicating Te = O was reduced to Te atoms. Impressively, the Te-CDs were stable after four oxidization-reduction circles as shown in Fig. 2c.

To investigate the generation of ROS of Te-CDs under irradiation, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used as a chemical trapping agent. Fig. 2d showed that the absorption peaks of DPBF system decreased gradually in 10 min under 808 nm irradiation (1.4 W cm−2) in presence of Te-CDs, which suggested that Te-CDs can produce ROS as photosensitizers under two-photon near-infrared excitation. The commercial photosensitizer agents, indocyanine green (ICG) was used for comparison. As shown in Fig. S9 (online) and Fig. 2e, although the ROS production of Te-CDs depends on two-photon absorption, the degradation rate of DPBF in Te-CDs system was close to the ICG system, which suggests it an alternative to ICG. At the same time, the temperature of Te-CDs solution only increased by 2.3 °C in 10 min under irradiation (Fig. S10 online), suggesting a low photothermal conversion efficiency. This observation showed that the Te-CDs is a superior photodynamic-only reagent for clinical applications [48]. Furthermore, the absorption of Te-CDs barely changed under 808 nm laser irradiation for 30 min (Fig. 2f), much more photo-stable than the classic organic dyes and inorganic counterparts such as fluorescein [49], porfimer sodium [50], tetramethyl rhodamine isothiocyanate (TRITC) [51] and gold nanorods [52].

Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was performed to further investigate the redox property of Te-CDs. The reagent 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) was employed as 1O2 spin-trapping agents. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the solution of H2O2 with TEMP exhibited characteristic 1:1:1 multiplicity ESR signals of 1O2. However, the intensity of signals decreased when Te-CDs were added into the solution, which revealed that Te-CDs can consume H2O2. Moreover, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as the ·OH spin-trapping agents to identify the type of ROS signal (Fig. 3b). When Te-CDs aqueous solution was under dark, no obvious ESR signals were observed. In comparison, when the solution was excited by 808 nm laser, the characteristic 1:2:2:1 multiplicity was detected, corresponding to DMPO/·OH adduct. For another two types of ROS, singlet oxygen and superoxide anion radicals, no signal change could be observed with or without laser irradiation (Fig. S12 online), which suggested that electrons transferred from Te-CDs to oxygen molecules, only yielding ·OH radicals under irradiation through electron transfer. We propose that with the help of valence band holes, ·OH are produced at Te-CDs surface by photocatalytic oxidation of H2O [1], while the energy transfer from the excited photocatalysts is inefficient due to mismatched energy levels of 3O2 and Te-CDs [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity, ROS scavenging and photodynamic application in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Te-CDs were incubated with L929 and HeLa cells to investigate the role of Te-CDs in vitro. Fig. 4 a showed that the viabilities of both cells remained above 80% even at the high concentration (200 μg mL−1) of Te-CDs, suggesting their excellent biocompatibility. When L929 cells were incubated with 5 ppm H2O2 for 24 h, half the cells were killed due to the oxidative stress (Fig. 4b). However, when L929 cells were incubated with both Te-CDs and H2O2, the viability of the cells is over 90%, indicating that Te-CDs can scavenge ROS to protect cells. Moreover, 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was used as a ROS fluorescence probe to estimate the oxidative stress level in L929 cells under different conditions (Fig. 4c). When the cells were incubated with 5 ppm H2O2, the fluorescence intensity was doubled, suggesting it can bring irreversible death of cells. When the cells were only incubated with Te-CDs, the fluorescence intensity slightly increased due to the intrinsic photoluminescence of Te-CDs. When the cells were incubated with both H2O2 and Te-CDs, the fluorescence intensity obviously decreased since Te-CDs can scavenge H2O2 to protect L929 cells, as is vividly shown in cell imaging pictures in Fig. S14 (online).

To investigate the photodynamic efficacy of Te-CDs in vitro, the cells were irradiated under 808 nm laser (1.4 W cm−2) for 10 min in the presence of Te-CDs (~125 μg mL−1), which showed over half Hela cells were killed (Fig. 4d), suggesting the strong phototoxicity of Te-CDs. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was performed to obtain vivid insight on this process. DCFH-DA can be internalized by HeLa cells and hydrolyzed to DCFH by intracellular esterase. As shown in Fig. 5 a, DCFH was oxidized by ROS to emit significant green fluorescence only in the group of Te-CDs after irradiation [54]. For other three groups, PBS- or laser- or Te-CDs-only groups showed negligible fluorescence owing to the absence of intracellular ROS. The four groups were also performed with calcein-AM (green fluorescence, excited at 488 nm) labeling live cells and propidium iodide (PI, red fluorescence, excited at 561 nm) labeling dead cells (Fig. 5b). No cell death was observed in PBS- or laser- or Te-CDs-only groups since only green fluorescence was observed, demonstrating the low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of Te-CDs. However, red fluorescence was visualized upon 808 nm laser irradiation (1.4 W cm−2, 10 min), which indicated parts of cells were killed by the generated ROS from Te-CDs.

In summary, a new kind of Te-CDs was synthesized and characterized, which is ultra-stable for H2O2 scavenge in the ambient situation and generating ·OH under laser irradiation. Combined these special properties with biocompatible carbon dots, both sides can be controlled via laser irradiation. In vitro study, Te-CDs can be employed in the field of cell protecting and photodynamic therapy. As excellent nanocarriers, Te-CDs promise to combine with other chemo-drugs or reagent to realize multi-modal therapy in the future. This contribution shed lights on designing novel materials with dual functions for ROS regulation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Foundation of China (21774130 and 51925306), National Key R&D Program of China (2018FYA 0305800), the Key Research Program of Frontier Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences (QYZDB-SSW-JSC046), Key Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (XDPB08-2), the Strategic Priority Research Program of Chinese Academy of Sciences (XDB28000000), the International Partnership Program of Chinese Academy of Sciences (211211KYSB20170014) and University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Hui Huang designed the experiments; Hao Chen carried out the experiments, analyzed the data and wrote the manuscript; Kaikai Wen contributed to the conception of the study; Jingya Chen helped perform the cell experiments; Wang Xing and Xiaoxi Wu contributed to the synthesis and characterizations of materials; Qinqin Shi and Aidong Peng helped perform the analysis with constructive discussions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary materials to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scib.2020.05.021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Nosaka Y, Nosaka AY. Generation and detection of reactive oxygen species in photocatalysis. Chem Rev 2017; 117: 11302-11336.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cheng W-W, Lin Z-Q, Wei B-F, et al. Single-walled carbon nanotube induction of rat aortic endothelial cell apoptosis: Reactive oxygen species are involved in the mitochondrial pathway. Int J Biochem Cell Biol 2011; 43: 564-572.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hao Chen received his B.E. degree at the University of Science and Technology Beijing in 2018. He is currently a Ph.D. student under the supervision of Prof. Hui Huang at the University of Chinese Academy of Sciences (UCAS). His research interests are related to the synthesis, properties and the application of organic optoelectrical materials.

Qinqin Shi is currently an Associated Professor at the College of Materials Science and Opto-electronic Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences. She obtained her Ph.D. degree from Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences under the supervision of Professor Xiaowei Zhan in 2011. From 2011 to 2013, she carried out her first postdoctoral training with Professor Seth R. Marder. And then under the mentoring of Professor Simon B. Blakey, she studied the C–H functionalization on the synthesis of semiconductors for 5 years. In 2019, she moved back to China and joined Hui Huang Team. Her current research interest focuses on the novel methodology studies of organic/polymeric semiconductors.

Aidong Peng received his Master’s degree from Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) in 2002, and then received his Ph.D. degree in 2005 from Institute of Chemistry, CAS, where he worked as an Assistant Professor and then an assosiate professor from 2005 to 2010. He is currently an Associate Professor of Materials Science at College of Materials Science and Opto-Electronic Technology, University of CAS. He is interested in organic and composite nanomaterials.

Hui Huang is currently a Professor at the College of Materials Science and Opto-electronic Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences (UCAS). He joined the UCAS in 2013. His current research interests include synthesis and applications of organic/polymeric semiconductors for photovoltaics, thin-film transistors (TFTs), photodetectors and biosensors.
